content:
Running real E2E tests before every commit — and the tricky problems we had to solve

9 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every team has that one developer who says “I’ll write tests later” — and somehow “later” never comes. We had a different problem: we had E2E tests in Playwright, but nobody was running them before pushing. The tests lived in a separate QA-Automation repo, the dev loop felt disconnected, and broken features were landing in commits regularly.
So we decided to wire Playwright directly into git commit using Husky — an interactive hook that pops up before every commit, lets you pick a test module, runs it with a real browser, and then asks what to do next.
This post covers exactly how we built it, every problem we hit, and every fix we shipped. If you’ve ever tried to do something “slightly unusual” with Git hooks, you’ll recognize these pain points.

What We Wanted
Before each git commit, the developer should:

• See an interactive terminal menu of available test modules

• Pick one (or skip entirely)

• Watch it run in a real browser

• See pass/fail — and decide whether to proceed

• Optionally run ESLint + Prettier on staged files

• Then commit (or abort)
No separate command to remember. No CI-only tests nobody runs locally. Just: commit → test → commit.
One extra constraint: our Playwright tests live in a separate repo (QA-Automation), not inside the main app repo. The hook had to bridge the two.

The Architecture
Before writing a line of code, it’s worth understanding the two-repo shape:
my-app/ ← your Angular app
.husky/pre-commit ← shell entry point (Git calls this)
scripts/run-playwright.mjs ← Node.js interactive runner
.env ← PLAYWRIGHT_TESTS_PATH lives here (gitignored)QA-Automation/ ← separate repo, anywhere on disk
specs/ ← test modules (auto-discovered)
tests/e2e/auth.setup.ts ← Auth0 login script
playwright/.auth/user.json ← saved session (gitignored)
playwright.hook.config.ts ← Playwright config for hook use
The my-app hook reaches into the QA-Automation folder at runtime using the path from the developer's local environment variable. Two repos, one workflow.
Tech Stack
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 1 — Install Husky and Wire the Hook
npm install --save-dev husky lint-staged @inquirer/selectnpx husky init
husky init creates .husky/pre-commit. We also added "prepare": "husky" to package.json — this makes Husky install automatically on every npm install, so new developers get the hook for free without any manual setup.

Step 2 — Reading the Test Path
The path to QA-Automation is stored in a local .env file (gitignored) that each developer sets once on their machine:
# .env — never committed
PLAYWRIGHT_TESTS_PATH=C:/Users/you/Documents/QA-Automation/In scripts/run-playwright.mjs, we read it using dotenv:
import { config } from 'dotenv';
import { resolve } from 'path';config(); // loads .env into process.env
const QA_DIR = resolve(process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_TESTS_PATH);
This keeps each developer’s local path out of source control entirely — nobody ever touches a committed file to set it up.

Step 3 — Building the Interactive Module Selector
The specs/ folder in QA-Automation has nested structure:
specs/
Document/
Labs/
Labs-Createorder/
Labs-Results/
Notes/
SmokeTesting/Note: The folder structure above is for reference only. You may organize your module folders differently based on your project’s requirements and conventions.
Some modules are flat, some have sub-modules. The menu needed to reflect both levels visually.
We built two functions:
// Check if a folder contains any .spec.ts files, recursively
function hasSpecs(dir) {
for (const entry of readdirSync(dir, { withFileTypes: true })) {
if (entry.isDirectory() && hasSpecs(join(dir, entry.name))) return true;
if (entry.isFile() && entry.name.endsWith('.spec.ts')) return true;
}
return false;
}// Build the choices array for @inquirer/select
function buildChoices(specsDir) {
const choices = [];
for (const entry of readdirSync(specsDir, { withFileTypes: true })) {
if (!entry.isDirectory()) continue;
const subPath = join(specsDir, entry.name);
const subDirs = readdirSync(subPath, { withFileTypes: true })
.filter(e => e.isDirectory() && hasSpecs(join(subPath, e.name)));
if (subDirs.length > 0) {
// Has sub-modules: show a group header + children
choices.push(new Separator(`── ${entry.name} ──`));
for (const sub of subDirs) {
choices.push({
name: ` ${entry.name} › ${sub.name}`,
value: `${entry.name}/${sub.name}`
});
}
} else if (hasSpecs(subPath)) {
choices.push({ name: entry.name, value: entry.name });
}
}
return choices;
}
Separator from @inquirer/select renders as a non-selectable visual divider — it gives the grouped layout without extra libraries.
🐛 The Import Mistake
import { select } from '@inquirer/select'; // ❌ WRONG@inquirer/select v5 switched to a default export. The correct import is:
import select, { Separator } from '@inquirer/select'; // ✅ CORRECTOne failed commit to find this. Always check breaking changes when a major version bumps.

Step 4 — The Shell Hook Problems
This is where things got genuinely tricky. The .husky/pre-commit script needs to:

• Run the Node script

• Handle the exit code

• Conditionally run lint-staged
Simple enough in theory. In practice, three problems hit us back-to-back.
Problem 1 — No TTY(Teletypewriter) in Git Hooks
@inquirer/select needs a real terminal to render the arrow-key interface. Git hooks don't attach stdin to the terminal — process.stdin.isTTY is false inside a hook.
The original script had this guard to handle non-TTY environments:
if (!process.stdin.isTTY) process.exit(0); // silently skips everythingSo when run via Husky, the whole prompt was being silently skipped and the commit went through untouched.
Fix: Remove the guard and redirect /dev/tty directly in the shell hook:
node scripts/run-playwright.mjs </dev/tty</dev/tty connects the real keyboard/terminal to the Node process's stdin. The interactive prompt renders perfectly after this one-line change.
Problem 2 — Husky Intercepts Non-Zero Exit Codes
The original design used exit codes to signal different outcomes:

• 0 = all good, run lint

• 1 = abort commit

• 2 = skip lint, commit as-is
node scripts/run-playwright.mjs </dev/tty
PW_EXIT=$?
if [ $PW_EXIT -eq 2 ]; then
exit 0 # user said "skip lint"
fi
npx lint-stagedThis never worked. Husky v9 wraps the hook and intercepts any non-zero exit code before the if block can act on it. Exit code 2 from Node was being swallowed by Husky and treated as a hook failure — the commit aborted instead of skipping lint.
Fix: Use a signal file instead of exit codes.

• Node writes .pw-hook-skip-lint when the user picks "skip lint"

• The shell hook checks for that file after Node exits cleanly with 0

• If the file exists: delete it and exit 0 (skip lint)

• If not: run npx lint-staged
node scripts/run-playwright.mjs </dev/tty
PW_EXIT=$?if [ $PW_EXIT -ne 0 ]; then
echo "✖ Commit aborted."
exit 1
fi
if [ -f ".pw-hook-skip-lint" ]; then
rm -f ".pw-hook-skip-lint"
echo "[Hook] Lint skipped. Committing now."
exit 0
fi
npx lint-staged
.pw-hook-skip-lint is gitignored so it can never accidentally land in a commit.
Problem 3 — UTF-8 BOM from PowerShell
The pre-commit file was initially written using PowerShell:
Set-Content .husky/pre-commit $content -Encoding UTF8PowerShell’s UTF8 mode adds a Byte Order Mark (BOM) — three invisible bytes (\357\273\277) at the start of the file. When Git Bash ran the hook, it tried to interpret those bytes as a command:
.husky/pre-commit: line 1: $'\357\273\277#': command not foundFix: Always write shell scripts using Bash’s cat heredoc, which writes UTF-8 without BOM:
cat <<'EOF' > .husky/pre-commit
#!/bin/sh
node scripts/run-playwright.mjs </dev/tty
# ... rest of hook
EOFRule of thumb: never use PowerShell to create shell scripts. Use Git Bash or any UTF-8 no-BOM editor (VS Code is fine).

Step 5 — The Playwright Config
The main playwright.config.ts in QA-Automation is built for CI — multiple workers, multiple browser projects, specific reporter paths. The hook needed something leaner.
We created playwright.hook.config.ts:
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';
import { existsSync } from 'fs';const REPORT_DIR = process.env.PW_REPORT_DIR ?? 'playwright-reports/default';
const AUTH_FILE = 'playwright/.auth/user.json';
const hasAuthSession = existsSync(AUTH_FILE);
export default defineConfig({
fullyParallel: false,
retries: 0,
workers: 1,
reporter: [['html', { outputFolder: REPORT_DIR, open: 'never' }]],
use: {
headless: false, // visible browser — developer can see what's happening
...(hasAuthSession ? { storageState: AUTH_FILE } : {}),
},
projects: [{
name: 'chromium',
use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] },
}],
});
The PW_REPORT_DIR env var is set by the runner script — each run gets a timestamped folder:
my-app/playwright-reports/Labs-Labs-Createorder-2026-06-23T10-17-58/Reports accumulate locally and are gitignored. Developers open index.html in a browser to review results.

Step 6 — Handling Auth0 Sessions
Some specs require a logged-in user. They don’t handle login themselves — they expect a valid storageState (cookies + localStorage from a previous Auth0 session saved to playwright/.auth/user.json).
When that file doesn’t exist, any auth-dependent spec immediately redirects to the Auth0 login page and fails.
First approach: Playwright project dependencies
We tried a conditional setup project in the config:
projects: [
...(hasAuthSession ? [] : [{ name: 'setup', testMatch: /auth\.setup\.ts/ }]),
{ name: 'chromium', dependencies: hasAuthSession ? [] : ['setup'] },
]This broke when a specific spec path was passed on the CLI — Playwright’s path filter excluded auth.setup.ts because it wasn't under specs/.
Final approach: sequential spawnSync in the runner script
Cleaner and more explicit — handle auth in Node before the spec runs:
import { spawnSync, existsSync } from 'child_process';
import { join } from 'path';const AUTH_FILE = join(QA_DIR, 'playwright', '.auth', 'user.json');
if (!existsSync(AUTH_FILE)) {
console.log('[Playwright] No auth session found — running login setup first...');
const authResult = spawnSync('npx', [
'playwright', 'test', 'tests/e2e/auth.setup.ts',
'--headed', '--config=playwright.hook.config.ts'
], { cwd: QA_DIR, shell: true, stdio: 'inherit' });
if (authResult.status !== 0) {
// Prompt: abort or skip
}
}
First commit: browser opens, developer logs in through Auth0, session is saved. The spec runs immediately after.
Every subsequent commit: user.json exists, auth step is skipped entirely — straight to the spec.
Session expired? Delete user.json and commit again. The hook re-runs login automatically.

Step 7 — Post-Run Prompts
After the spec runs, the user needs context-aware options:
Tests passed:
✅ Run ESLint + Prettier ← recommended
⏭ Skip lint — commit as-isTests failed:
🚫 Abort commit ← fix tests first
➡️ Continue — run ESLint + Prettier anyway
⏭ Skip everything — commit as-isThe “continue despite failure” option matters in practice. Sometimes the test itself is broken, not the code being committed. Forcing an abort in that case blocks legitimate work. The hook informs and lets the developer decide — it doesn’t try to be smarter than the person using it.

The Full Commit Flow
git commit
│
▼
[Hook] Arrow-key module selector appears
│
├─ "Skip Playwright" → goes straight to lint-staged
│
└─ Developer picks a module (e.g. Labs › Labs-Createorder)
│
▼
Check playwright/.auth/user.json
│
├─ Missing → run auth.setup.ts (browser opens, login once)
│
└─ Found → skip login, go straight to tests
│
▼
Spec runs in headed Chromium
Report saved → playwright-reports/<module>-<timestamp>/
│
├─ PASSED → prompt: run lint | skip lint
│
└─ FAILED → prompt: abort | run lint anyway | skip everything
Key Takeaways
1. Signal files beat exit codes when Husky is in the way. Husky v9 owns the exit code channel. If you need to communic
